platform.name: Keycloak
platform.organization: Keycloak project, Cloud Native Computing Foundation

surface.name: Docker getting-started guide through first application sign-in
surface.selection_basis: Keycloak publishes separate getting-started guides for OpenJDK, Docker, Podman, Kubernetes, and OpenShift. This record uses the official Docker guide because it is a self-contained development path that continues beyond server startup through realm and user creation, client registration, and authentication to the supplied SPA test application. The deployment-platform alternatives are preserved as branches.

alternatives_considered.name: OpenJDK getting started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires a local JDK and archive installation before following the same administrative concepts.

alternatives_considered.name: Podman getting started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a peer container-runtime path rather than the selected Docker path.

alternatives_considered.name: Kubernetes getting started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It adds cluster and Kubernetes deployment prerequisites.

alternatives_considered.name: OpenShift getting started
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It adds OpenShift access and deployment-specific actions.

entry_point.developer_intent: Start Keycloak locally, create the first realm and user, register the first OpenID Connect client, and authenticate to the supplied test application.
assumed_prior_state: The developer has not started a Keycloak server.
The developer has Docker but no existing Keycloak realm, user, or client.
The development-only admin credentials and local HTTP endpoints from the guide are acceptable for the exercise.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: Confirm the first client was created successfully by signing in to the Keycloak SPA testing application.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: A developer starts Keycloak, creates myrealm and myuser, registers the myclient OpenID Connect client, and authenticates that user to Keycloak's supplied SPA test application.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The SPA testing application completes Sign in through the locally running Keycloak server with myuser.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The guide says to confirm the client was created successfully with the SPA testing application, then ends the setup path after Click Sign in to authenticate to this application.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: The sign-in test is the final verification action in Secure the first application. The next heading is Taking the next step and moves to production database, TLS, and admin-password recommendations.

prerequisites.requirement: Docker installed and able to run Linux container images.
Sufficient machine or container-platform memory and CPU for Keycloak.
Local port 8080 available and network access to pull quay.io/keycloak/keycloak:26.7.0.
A browser that can reach localhost:8080 and https://www.keycloak.org/app/.

primary_path.action: Open Keycloak's Docker getting-started guide.
details: The guide is one of five platform-specific getting-started routes listed by Keycloak.
primary_path.input: Intent to try Keycloak with Docker.
primary_path.output: Ordered Docker setup and first-application instructions.
primary_path.success_signal: Before you start and Start Keycloak are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing a different deployment platform changes the startup prerequisites and commands.

primary_path.action: Confirm Docker is installed and that the machine has sufficient resources.
details: The guide requires Docker and directs production sizing questions to the sizing guidance.
primary_path.input: Supported machine with Docker installation privileges.
primary_path.success_signal: The docker command is available and the Docker daemon is running.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Docker installation and machine sizing are not decomposed in this guide.

primary_path.action: Start Keycloak 26.7.0 in development mode with bootstrap administrator credentials.
details: Run docker run with 127.0.0.1:8080 mapped to container port 8080.
Set KC_BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_USERNAME=admin and KC_BOOTSTRAP_ADMIN_PASSWORD=admin.
Use quay.io/keycloak/keycloak:26.7.0 start-dev.
primary_path.input: Docker, local port 8080, image registry access, and development admin values.
primary_path.output: Running local Keycloak server and initial admin user.
primary_path.success_signal: Keycloak is exposed at local port 8080.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The first run may need to pull the container image. Image, port, or resource failures prevent the Admin Console from opening.

primary_path.action: Open the Keycloak Admin Console at localhost:8080.
primary_path.success_signal: The login form loads.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If it does not load, the server or port mapping is not ready.

primary_path.action: Log in with administrator username admin and password admin.
details: These values were created by the Docker command.
primary_path.output: Authenticated Admin Console session in the master realm.
primary_path.success_signal: The Admin Console displays.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Incorrect environment values or credentials cause login failure.

primary_path.action: Click Manage realms in the left column.
details: The master realm is only for managing Keycloak, not applications.
primary_path.success_signal: Create realm is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Create realm.
primary_path.success_signal: Realm name can be entered.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Enter myrealm in the Realm name field.
details: A realm is equivalent to a tenant and isolates applications and users.
primary_path.success_signal: The Realm name field contains myrealm.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Create to create myrealm.
primary_path.success_signal: The current realm switches to myrealm.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Verify that myrealm appears next to Current realm.
details: The guide requires remaining user actions to occur in myrealm.
primary_path.success_signal: myrealm is the current realm.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If another realm is current, switch to myrealm before continuing.

primary_path.action: Click Users in the left-hand menu.
details: A new realm initially has no users.
primary_path.success_signal: Create new user is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Create new user.
primary_path.success_signal: User fields are displayed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Fill in the user form.
details: Set Username to myuser.
Enter any first name.
Enter any last name.
primary_path.input: Username and chosen first and last names.
primary_path.success_signal: All three documented fields have values.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Create to create myuser.
primary_path.success_signal: The created user's detail page opens.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Credentials at the top of the user's page.
details: The user needs a password before login.
primary_path.success_signal: Set password form is visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Enter a password in the Set password form.
details: The guide leaves the password value to the developer.
primary_path.output: Password value ready to be set.
primary_path.success_signal: The password fields are populated.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Password policy behavior is not described in the guide.

primary_path.action: Toggle Temporary to Off.
details: This prevents a forced password update at first login.
primary_path.success_signal: Temporary is Off and the password is set for first login.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The guide does not separately name the form-submission control after this toggle.

primary_path.action: Open the Keycloak Account Console at localhost:8080.
details: This is an intermediate check that the user was configured correctly.
primary_path.success_signal: The Account Console prompts for authentication.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If unavailable, verify the server is still running.

primary_path.action: Log in to the Account Console with myuser and the password just created.
primary_path.success_signal: The user's account-management page opens.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Incorrect password, realm, or incomplete password setting blocks login.

primary_path.action: Verify that the user can access the Account Console.
details: The page lets the user manage profile, two-factor authentication, and identity-provider accounts.
primary_path.success_signal: Account-management options are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A failed login indicates the user or password configuration must be corrected.

primary_path.action: Return to or reopen the Keycloak Admin Console.
primary_path.success_signal: Administrative navigation is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Reauthenticate as admin if the session ended.

primary_path.action: Select myrealm next to Current realm.
details: The first application must be registered in myrealm, not master.
primary_path.success_signal: myrealm is shown as current.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Registering in another realm changes the issuer and users available to the client.

primary_path.action: Click Clients.
primary_path.success_signal: Create client is available.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Create client.
primary_path.success_signal: Client type and Client ID fields appear.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Set Client type to OpenID Connect and Client ID to myclient.
primary_path.success_signal: OpenID Connect and myclient are displayed.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Click Next.
primary_path.success_signal: Standard flow setting is visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Confirm that Standard flow is enabled.
details: The test application uses the standard OpenID Connect browser flow.
primary_path.success_signal: The Standard flow control is enabled.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Enable it if it is not already enabled.

primary_path.action: Click Next.
primary_path.success_signal: Valid redirect URIs and Web origins fields are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: None documented.

primary_path.action: Set the client login URLs for the supplied test application.
details: Set Valid redirect URIs to https://www.keycloak.org/app/*.
Set Web origins to https://www.keycloak.org.
primary_path.success_signal: Both URL fields match the guide.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Incorrect URLs prevent the browser application from completing the flow.

primary_path.action: Click Save to create the client.
primary_path.success_signal: The client settings are saved.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Validation errors or incorrect fields must be corrected.

primary_path.action: Open the SPA testing application at https://www.keycloak.org/app/.
details: The guide uses this application to confirm the client registration.
primary_path.input: Browser access to the Keycloak website.
primary_path.success_signal: The test application's configuration interface loads.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Network or browser policy can block the external test application.

primary_path.action: Click Save to use the test application's default configuration.
details: The client values created in the guide are designed for the supplied defaults.
primary_path.success_signal: The Sign in action becomes available with the saved configuration.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If defaults were changed, they must point to the local myrealm and myclient configuration.

primary_path.action: Click Sign in and authenticate through the local Keycloak server as myuser.
details: Use the myuser password created earlier when the Keycloak login flow requests it.
primary_path.input: Saved SPA configuration and myuser credentials.
primary_path.success_signal: The sign-in flow returns to the SPA as the authenticated user.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A stopped server, mismatched realm/client URL, invalid redirect or origin, or incorrect user credentials prevents completion.

primary_path.action: Confirm that the first client successfully authenticated the test application.
details: This is the guide's final verification before Taking the next step.
primary_path.output: Verified realm, user, and OpenID Connect client path.
primary_path.success_signal: The SPA displays its authenticated state after returning from Keycloak.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The guide does not decompose the test application's post-login screen or error messages.

branches.condition: The developer does not use Docker.
branches.path: Select Keycloak's OpenJDK, Podman, Kubernetes, or OpenShift getting-started guide, complete its platform-specific server startup, then perform the equivalent realm, user, client, and sign-in actions.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes installation, deployment, resource, and access steps while retaining the first authenticated-client concept.

branches.condition: The server is not a disposable local development instance.
branches.path: Use production mode, a production database, TLS certificates, secure bootstrap credentials, and the relevant deployment guide.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds infrastructure, security, database, hostname, and certificate work before sign-in.

branches.path: The user must update the password during first login before reaching the Account Console or test application.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an end-user credential-change form and another authentication interaction.

branches.condition: The developer uses their own application instead of Keycloak's SPA tester.
branches.path: Configure that application's redirect URI and origin, integrate an appropriate OpenID Connect library or adapter, initiate sign-in, handle the redirect, and verify authenticated state.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds application code and framework-specific integration beyond the supplied test client.

friction_gates.description: Docker must be installed before the selected getting-started route can start Keycloak.
The host must provide sufficient memory and CPU, and local port 8080 must be usable.
Development bootstrap administrator credentials are passed as environment variables and grant administrative access.
The realm user needs a password before either documented login verification can succeed.
Temporary must be turned Off to avoid a forced first-login password change in the documented path.
The redirect URI, web origin, realm, client ID, and standard flow must match the external SPA test application.
The browser must reach both the public Keycloak test application and the locally running identity server.

sources.title: Docker: Get started with Keycloak on Docker
sections_used: Log in to the Admin Console
Log in to the Account Console

sources.title: Running Keycloak in a container
sections_used: Provide initial admin credentials when running in a container

uncertainties.question: Which exact control submits the Set password form after the password is entered and Temporary is toggled Off?
uncertainties.impact: The guide may omit one UI click in the user-credential sequence.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The current guide lists entering the password and toggling Temporary but does not name a separate submit button in the text.

uncertainties.question: What exact post-login content proves success in the current SPA testing application?
uncertainties.impact: The final observable signal can be described only as returned authenticated state, not a precise label or field.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The guide ends at Click Sign in and does not enumerate the external test application's credential prompts, callback screen, or error states.

uncertainties.question: Does the first docker run need to download the Keycloak image?
uncertainties.impact: A registry download adds an unbounded wait before the server starts.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: It depends on the workstation's local image cache.

excluded_after_success: Switching to a production-ready database such as PostgreSQL.
Production hostname, proxy, clustering, monitoring, and hardening.
Integrating a developer-owned application or framework-specific adapter.
Stopping or removing the local development container.
